old_claim.note: Computerized Tomography (CT/MRI) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: MRI is an imaging application of nuclear magnetic resonance using magnetic fields and radio-frequency signals.

source_shape.source_locator: Nobel 2003 press-release sections describing NMR, magnetic fields, radio waves, and Lauterbur/Mansfield imaging.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Nobel release explains that MRI uses nuclear magnetic resonance signals from atomic nuclei in magnetic fields and credits Lauterbur and Mansfield for turning NMR into imaging.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports NMR as the required foundation because the source describes MRI as the imaging use of NMR, while it does not make CT part of the MRI method.

ontology_before: MRI was made dependent on a synthetic CT/MRI bundle, incorrectly implying CT-like imaging was a foundation for MRI.
ontology_after: MRI now depends directly on nuclear magnetic resonance, with computing and signal processing as reconstruction enablers.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the false CT/MRI bundle edge is absent. Preserved: MRI remains connected to imaging reconstruction through computing and DSP.
would_reject_if: A source showed MRI requires computed tomography as a method prerequisite.
The graph reintroduced computerized_tomography_ct_mri as a direct MRI prerequisite.
The NMR node were removed without another source-backed NMR foundation replacing it.
short_reason: MRI is built on nuclear magnetic resonance, not on computed tomography.
